narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model estimated a 57% chance that you would not pass the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This threshold triggered the rejection to help the school maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on three key factors from your profile: your LSAT score, your third-year grade decile, and your first-year grade decile. Above all, your LSAT score of 25 stood out as the biggest push toward predicting failure—it's notably lower than the average of about 37 for students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential challenge in handling the intense legal reasoning tested later. On a brighter note, your third-year decile of 4 worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it aligns closer to the typical 5.9 average for bar passers, showing real improvement over time. Your first-year decile of 1, however, nudged things back toward failure, as it's much lower than the 6 average for those who succeeded, highlighting an early struggle that the model weighed against you. While your upper-middle family income fits well within the majority of passers and your part-time status is less common but not decisive here, these academic markers dominated the thinking. Compared to typical accepted students—who often enter with LSATs around 37, undergraduate GPAs near 3.24 like yours at 2.4 but bolstered by stronger early deciles—your profile appeared riskier overall. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, just the model's read on bar success patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your early academic record through additional undergrad courses or targeted prep, and reapplying with evidence of upward trends like your third-year gains. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these exact areas, and admissions teams value persistence. You're already showing growth—build on that.
